macro_segments.title: Sins and Their Corresponding Punishments: Theft, Injustice, and Oath-Breaking

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the discourse on why women die in childbirth and theodicy
The sin of theft leads to locusts and famine; the example of the women of Mechuza
Four types of judicial sin: oppressing justice, perverting justice, corrupting justice, and neglecting Torah
The sin of false oaths and oaths for naught lead to evil beasts and desolate pathways

macro_segments.title: Sins Leading to Exile, Destruction, and the Connection Between Sin and Suffering

micro_segments.title: Murder leads to the destruction of the Beit HaMikdash and the departure of the Divine Presence
Sexual immorality, idolatry, and violation of Sabbatical years lead to exile and dispossession

macro_segments.title: Foul Speech (Nivlut Peh) as a Sin: Consequences and the Problem of Innocent Suffering

micro_segments.title: The sin of foul speech brings new afflictions, death of youths, and suffering of orphans and widows
Discussion of Rav Chanan bar Rava on foul speech: even a seventy-year decree of good is reversed to evil
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak's reversal: using illness as a sign of sin; the problem of theodicy
Three types of hidrokan (intestinal disease) based on their cause: sin, famine, or witchcraft

macro_segments.title: The Meeting at Kerem b'Yavne: Askara, Lashon Hara, and the Problem of Punishing the Innocent

micro_segments.title: Why does askara (respiratory illness) start in the intestines and end in the mouth? The assembly at Kerem b'Yavne
Rabbi Yehuda explains the progression of askara mirrors the progression of sin from internal faculties to speech
Three explanations for askara: lashon hara, eating non-kosher food (tevel), and neglect of Torah study
The challenge of theodicy: how do women, non-Jews, and young children get askara if they aren't responsible for the attributed sins?
Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai's answer: the righteous suffer for the generation; the innocent (tinokot shel beit rabban) suffer when there are no righteous to protect the community

macro_segments.title: The Story of Rabbi Yehuda ben Gerim and the Roman Infrastructure: Attitudes Toward Non-Jewish Civilization

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda praises Roman accomplishments (marketplaces, bridges, bathhouses); Rabbi Yose remains silent; Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai condemns them as self-serving
Rabbi Yehuda ben Gerim reports the conversation; the government exiles Rabbi Yose and executes Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai in the Cave: Learning and Reintegration into the World

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon and his son hide in a cave for twelve years; sustained by a carob tree and a spring of water
They emerge and burn people engaged in earthly pursuits with their gaze; a heavenly voice sends them back for twelve months
Final emergence: they see an old man with myrtle branches preparing for Shabbat, teaching them to appreciate the commandments of Israel

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Tikun (Rectification) for His Community: Purifying the Cemetery

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon decides to benefit his community like Yaakov did for Shechem
He identifies and marks graves in a cemetery causing difficulty for priests; an elder critiques him for purifying a cemetery
Rabbi Shimon rebukes the elder for attacking a colleague; the elder dies from the evil eye
Rabbi Shimon sees Rabbi Yehuda ben Gerim in the marketplace and kills him with the evil eye; end of daf

topical_tags.term: Theodicy and Why Bad Things Happen
Tinokot Shel Beit Rabban (Children in School)
Cave Story: Mystical Learning and Isolation
Midrashic Connection Between Sin and Punishment
